statement.markdown: Given two strings `s` and `t`, return the minimum window substring of `s` that contains all the characters of `t`. If there is no such window, return an empty string. The window should be as short as possible.
statement.inputFormat: Two strings `s` and `t`, where `s` is the main string and `t` is the string that must be contained in the minimum window of `s`.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum window substring of `s` that contains all the characters of `t`, or an empty string if no such window exists.
statement.notes: The window should be as short as possible.

constraints.description: The string that must be contained in the minimum window of `s`

examples.explanation: The minimum window substring of `s` that contains all the characters of `t` is "BANC"
There is no window in `s` that contains all the characters of `t`

hints.text: Use a frequency counter to track the characters in `t` and the current window of `s`

editorial.title: Minimum Window Substring
editorial.intuition: We can use a frequency counter to track the characters in `t` and the current window of `s`.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: try all possible substrings of `s` and check if they contain all the characters of `t`
editorial.betterSolution: Use a frequency counter to track the characters in `t` and the current window of `s`
editorial.optimalSolution: Maintain two pointers, one at the start of the window and one at the end. Use the frequency counter to check if the current window contains all the characters of `t`. If it does, update the minimum window and move the start pointer forward.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution works because it maintains a minimum window that contains all the characters of `t` and iterates over all possible windows of `s`.

functionSignature.java: public String minWindow(String s, String t);
functionSignature.python: def minWindow(self, s: str, t: str) -> str:

testCases.input:
Enter s: ADOBECODEBANC
Enter t: ABC

Enter s: a
Enter t: aa
